tasks.title: Complete Button/Input/FormField primitive normalization in Knit
tasks.description: Finish Stage 1 of the Knit primitive replacement wave. The remaining high-use Knit flows in SidebarContent and settings still need to adopt Looma Button, Input, Select, and FormField primitives. This is listed as 'in progress' in the migration inventory.
outOfScope: New Looma components not already shipped

acceptanceCriteria.description: SidebarContent.vue uses Looma Button, Input, Select, FormField in all dialog-heavy flows
settings.vue uses Looma primitives for all form controls
pnpm typecheck passes in Knit
pnpm build passes in Knit
looma-migration-inventory.md updated to reflect completed status for affected surfaces

tasks.title: Promote AvatarGroup from Knit to Looma
tasks.description: Extract the AvatarGroup/AvatarStack pattern from Knit's atoms/AvatarGroup.vue into a generic Looma component. Presence-specific data binding must stay in Knit; only the generic presentation layer moves to Looma. Per the migration inventory this is a B-category promotion candidate.
outOfScope: Any Knit-specific avatar naming or workspace vocabulary in the API

acceptanceCriteria.description: Generic ui-avatar-group component ships in @looma/core with domain-neutral API (no workspace/presence vocabulary)
React and Vue adapter exports exist for AvatarGroup
Contract README, Docusaurus page, and Storybook story all ship in the same change
pnpm generate:api && pnpm check:docs-sync pass
Knit's AvatarGroup.vue is updated to consume the new Looma primitive

tasks.title: Fix open editor bugs blocking Knit dogfooding
tasks.description: There are open shared editor defects found through Knit dogfooding documented in looma/docs/editor-bugs.md. These are blocking broader editor UI migration. Read editor-bugs.md and fix the listed defects in @looma/editor.
outOfScope: New editor features not in editor-roadmap.md

acceptanceCriteria.description: Each bug listed in docs/editor-bugs.md is either fixed or explicitly deferred with rationale
pnpm --filter @looma/editor typecheck passes
pnpm --filter @looma/editor build passes
docs/editor-bugs.md updated with fix status for each item

tasks.title: Implement inline comments — schema exists, API + UI to do
tasks.description: The comments table already exists in the Supabase schema. This task implements the API endpoints and UI for inline/page comments: select text → add comment, thread replies, show comments on page. This is item #5 in the Knit feature roadmap parity list.
outOfScope: Any changes to the comments table schema

acceptanceCriteria.description: User can select text in the editor and add a comment
Comments appear in a sidebar or inline indicator on the page
Thread replies work (add reply, display thread)
server/api routes for comment CRUD exist and are protected by workspace membership
pnpm typecheck passes

tasks.title: Add unit tests for use-collections, use-presence, and subdomain utils
tasks.description: V1 polish item from PROJECT_STATE.md. These three composables/utils have no unit tests. Write vitest tests covering the main behaviours: collection tree reactivity, presence channel lifecycle, and subdomain slug extraction/construction.

acceptanceCriteria.description: use-collections has tests covering: initial load, create, optimistic reorder, and revert on error
subdomain utils has tests covering: extractWorkspaceSlug and workspaceHost for valid, invalid, and edge-case inputs
use-presence has tests covering: channel join, presence sync, and leave
pnpm test passes

tasks.title: Generate TypeScript types from Supabase schema
tasks.description: Run pnpm db:types to generate proper TypeScript types from the Supabase schema and wire them into the codebase. Currently Knit uses ad-hoc types. This is a V1 polish item and will improve type safety across all server API routes and composables.
outOfScope: Migrating all files to use generated types in one pass (incremental adoption is fine)

acceptanceCriteria.description: Generated types file exists at the expected output path after running pnpm db:types
At least the server/api and composables directories import from generated types where the types overlap with existing ad-hoc types
pnpm typecheck passes after adoption

tasks.title: Reduce workspace-import noise and preserve subproject scope
tasks.description: Tighten workspace import so recursive repo scanning produces a sane backlog. Preserve subproject identity from nested docs, skip obvious formatting junk, and make the detector/hypothesis layer emit task candidates that belong to the right project area instead of a flat core bucket.
outOfScope: General UI polish outside import review surfaces

acceptanceCriteria.description: Workspace import draft preserves subproject/domain hints from nested planning docs
Obvious formatting junk and placeholder signals are filtered before draft tasks are created
Focused workspace-import hypothesis tests cover domain preservation and junk filtering

notes.content: Completed importer cleanup pass: defaulted nested doc scanning to single-project unless multiple roots are clearly present, preserved real subproject hints for Looma/Knit, filtered migration-guide/spec-template/section-header junk, and fuzzily deduped near-identical planning tasks from the same file. Live Looma/Knit import draft improved from 677/583 to 312/219 (input/drafted).

tasks.title: Shape importer output into a usable Guildhall backlog
tasks.description: Collapse raw workspace-import signals into backlog units a human can approve quickly: task-sized work, milestone summaries, and context docs without overwhelming quantity.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Workspace import draft groups work into sane task-sized backlog items instead of raw fragment lists
Imported preview clearly distinguishes backlog tasks, milestones, and context

notes.content: Activated after automation-001. Remaining focus: collapse narrative/comparative roadmap bullets into backlog units a human can approve quickly, and keep task/context/milestone boundaries crisp in workspace-import draft output.

tasks.title: Get one real task from intake to spec review without manual cleanup
tasks.description: Prove that a grounded project task can move from fuzzy ask through spec shaping and land in clean spec_review or a clean waiting-on-user state without silent loops or stale task residue.

acceptanceCriteria.description: A real imported task consistently lands in spec_review or a clear user-question state with durable transcript and task state

notes.content: Grounded Looma/Knit intake now lands cleanly without manual cleanup. The spec lane preserves durable progress after turn-limit churn, answered questions no longer strand tasks in exploring, and task-006 moved from a fresh ask through structured questions into spec_review on the live server.

tasks.title: Run implementation, review, and gates against real project truth
tasks.description: Make Guildhall finish work against the target project's actual verification contract. Tasks should fail or block for real project reasons, not weak bootstrap stand-ins or stale runtime assumptions.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Task completion uses real project-specific gates where they exist
Guildhall surfaces explicit blocked-state reasons for unrelated repo failures or missing human input

notes.content: Live Looma/Knit gate-check proved the next real blocker is task-vs-project gate truth. Guildhall is now being updated so explicit automated acceptance-criteria commands become the authoritative hard gates for their category, with broader project defaults only filling in missing categories.

Task-scoped automated gates now narrow the live run correctly, and pnpm commands get basic normalization before they become hard gates. The remaining blocker is command trust plus escalation hygiene: stale open gate escalations can stop a rerun before gate_check replays, and some agent-authored commands still need runtime validation against the repo layout before they should be treated as authoritative.

Stale unresolved escalations no longer block reruns once the task has clearly moved forward after the escalation was raised and is no longer in blocked status. The same supersede rule now drives the orchestrator, inbox, thread, and stuck-state UI so false Needs-you noise drops with the runtime fix.

Live Looma/Knit task-009 now completed through gate_check under the hardened task-scoped gate contract. Guildhall persisted the done transition cleanly after narrowing the test gate to the targeted callback file, invalidating stale gate-check sessions, and letting update-task infer current_task_id from runtime metadata.

tasks.title: Automate the PR and merge path for completed tasks
tasks.description: Once a task is genuinely done, Guildhall should be able to push branch state through review packaging and merge without human bookkeeping.
outOfScope: Batch queue scheduling beyond one task at a time

acceptanceCriteria.description: One real task reaches a merged state without manual PR bookkeeping

notes.content: Automation-004 is now effectively satisfied by a real Looma/Knit task reaching done under real narrowed gates. The next autonomy gap is PR and merge packaging: inspect Guildhall's done-task publication path and make one grounded task move through branch/PR bookkeeping without manual cleanup.

The next publication blocker is now crisp: task-009 reached done under real narrowed gates, but Looma/Knit did not allocate a task branch/worktree, so there was no mergeRecord and no PR path to run. Guildhall now records an explicit skipped merge when publication was never attempted; the next slice is enabling or truthfully configuring the branch/publication lane for a real project.

The next publication blocker is now sharper and partially cleared: the multi-repo workspace root was never a git repo, so Guildhall was pointing worktree and merge operations at the wrong path for Looma/Knit tasks. The runtime now routes branch/worktree/merge cleanup through the task's effective project repo (for example `knit/`), which makes a real PR/merge proof possible on the next grounded subproject task.

Publication hardening now covers the next truthful blocker too: if a target subrepo is already dirty when worktree isolation is on, Guildhall stops before minting a task branch and surfaces an explicit repo-dirty worktree error. For Looma/Knit, that means the next live publication proof needs either a clean knit/ repo or an intentional publication pass for the already-dirty auth work.

Publication proof now exists for a grounded Looma/Knit task. Task-006 ran in a dedicated task worktree on knit/, replayed through narrowed task-scoped gates, and recorded a successful local ff-only merge record back to main without manual PR bookkeeping.

tasks.title: Scale from one-task autonomy to unattended queue throughput
tasks.description: After one end-to-end success, teach Guildhall to continue through multiple tasks, skip blocked work cleanly, and stop only when the queue is exhausted or globally blocked.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Guildhall can continue through multiple unblocked tasks without manual restarts between each one
Blocked tasks are explicitly surfaced and skipped instead of stalling the whole queue on first contact

notes.content: Before scaling unattended queue throughput, gate execution now enforces the authoritative task-scoped gate list at tool runtime. This prevents one bad model turn from reintroducing broad or stale commands and poisoning the historical pass/fail record.

tasks.title: Reframe provider UX around authenticated CLIs and protocol families
tasks.description: Replace product-specific provider framing with a protocol-first model: authenticated CLIs, OpenAI-compatible custom providers, and Anthropic-compatible custom providers. Product names like LM Studio should become examples or saved connection labels, not top-level runtime concepts.
outOfScope: Multi-connection persistence beyond the first safe slice

acceptanceCriteria.description: User-facing docs and setup/provider surfaces describe provider families without depending on LM Studio as a first-class product type
Guildhall has a committed design note describing the protocol-first provider taxonomy and migration path

notes.content: Completed the provider taxonomy cleanup slice. Added a protocol-first design note, reframed the setup/providers UI and docs around authenticated CLIs plus OpenAI-compatible / Anthropic-compatible providers, changed the local-provider language from LM Studio-as-concept to OpenAI-compatible local server, and kept existing config keys compatible.

tasks.title: Normalize effective provider runtime config across every lane
tasks.description: Use one canonical runtime resolver for preflight, orchestrator lanes, provider tests, and UI status so provider family, base URL, auth source, model preset, fallback policy, and capability hints stay aligned.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Preflight, worker/reviewer/coordinator dispatch, provider test actions, and project-status UI all resolve the same effective provider runtime record
Focused tests cover provider-family resolution, protocol-specific defaults, and fallback consistency

notes.content: Started runtime normalization by adding shared provider metadata, provider-family classification, a normalized provider-name alias path, and a preferredProviderFamily field on RuntimeProviderConfig. Remaining work is to drive that effective runtime record more explicitly through all status/reporting surfaces and capability routing.

Extended the normalized runtime record into the serve-layer status path. /api/project and live run providerStatus now include provider family and label fields for both preferred and active providers, and the Project view can consume those directly instead of re-deriving display labels ad hoc.

Finished the runtime-normalization slice by routing reviewer fanout through the same provider-aware policy helper in both orchestrator dispatch and provider-status snapshots. Reviewer lane concurrency is now resolved once from provider capabilities and surfaced back to the UI as requested vs effective concurrency.

tasks.title: Add provider capability manifests for routing and explainability
tasks.description: Represent provider abilities and limits explicitly so lane routing, fallback, and UI status can rely on capability truth instead of hand-coded provider names.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Resolved provider runtime includes a compact capability manifest covering streaming, tool calls, reasoning behavior, resumable sessions, and recommended concurrency
Project/task UI can surface why a provider was selected or rejected using capability-based reasons

notes.content: Started the provider capability-manifest slice. Added a static capability record per provider family/key and threaded preferred/active capability snapshots into RuntimeProviderConfig, ProviderRunStatus, /api/project, and the UI types. This is groundwork for capability-based routing and clearer operator messaging.

Capability manifests now affect real runtime behavior: reviewer fanout is clamped through a shared provider-aware policy, and provider status explains requested vs effective reviewer concurrency instead of only issuing a generic warning.

Finished the provider-capability slice by replacing loose fallback reason strings with structured routing decisions in provider status. The Project view now surfaces capability- and compatibility-based provider selection decisions directly, so operators can see why a provider was chosen or rejected.

tasks.title: Add a shared provider client pool with bounded concurrency
tasks.description: Create pooled transport clients keyed by normalized provider runtime identity so worker, reviewer, and coordinator agents reuse connections and respect concurrency limits centrally.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Equivalent lane dispatches reuse pooled provider clients instead of constructing ad hoc transport instances
The pool enforces provider-level concurrency and exposes provider-health events for repeated transient/fatal failures

notes.content: Next slice is the shared provider client pool. Capability-based runtime identity is now stable enough that we can key pooled transport clients by normalized provider runtime instead of provider-name special cases.

Started the client-pool implementation with a module-local provider client pool. Equivalent OpenAI-compatible and local-server runtime configs now reuse pooled clients, while Claude OAuth intentionally stays unpooled for now because it carries resumable session identity that should not bleed across runs.

Added provider-health tracking at the pool boundary. Pooled clients now record last success/failure, consecutive failures, retryable vs fatal counts, and degraded state; provider status can surface that health and the Project view warns when a pooled provider is degrading.

Provider health now propagates into the live runtime surface. The pool publishes health-change notifications, the supervisor maps them onto matching runs, and the workspace event stream emits provider_health_changed events so active runs can refresh degraded-provider state without waiting for a manual reload.

Degraded pooled clients now affect runtime behavior instead of just observability. After repeated retryable failures or any fatal failure, the next equivalent pooled acquisition recycles the stateless client so Guildhall stops reusing a poisoned transport instance.

Finished the shared-client-pool slice by enforcing provider-level concurrency inside pooled stateless clients. The pool now combines reuse, bounded concurrency, health tracking, live provider_health_changed events, and degraded-client recycling in one normalized layer.

tasks.title: Add bounded lane scheduling on top of the one-task kernel
tasks.description: Grow the one-task finisher into a bounded multi-lane scheduler: a narrow spec lane, a capped worker lane, bounded review/gate fanout, and a serialized coordinator lane.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Queue execution can run more than one eligible task at a time without violating worktree-slot ownership or reviewer/gate ordering guarantees
Lane-level concurrency settings exist for spec, worker, review, and coordinator lanes

notes.content: With provider runtime normalization and the shared client pool in place, the next slice is bounded lane scheduling. The scheduler can now assume provider reuse/concurrency/health are handled below it and focus on task-lane policy rather than transport plumbing.

Finished the bounded-lane slice. Guildhall now resolves explicit spec/worker/review/coordinator lane settings from project config, clamps them against dispatch capacity and provider policy, uses those budgets during task selection, and surfaces the effective lane plan in provider status.

tasks.title: Prove unattended throughput in staged queue runs
tasks.description: After provider/runtime and lane scheduling are trustworthy, prove Guildhall can continue through a queue in stages: one real task merged, three tasks processed without manual restart, then a longer run that stops only when blocked or exhausted.

acceptanceCriteria.description: Guildhall can process at least three real unblocked tasks in one unattended run with explicit blocked accounting
Longer queue runs stop with a truthful exhausted-or-blocked summary instead of silent stalls

notes.content: Lane scheduling is now explicit and test-covered. The next stage is proving staged unattended throughput: one grounded task merged cleanly, then a short bounded multi-task run that stops with explicit blocked/exhausted accounting instead of silent drift.

Added an explicit stop-summary contract for unattended runs. Idle shutdown now explains whether the queue is all terminal, waiting on humans, blocked on escalations, or dependency-stalled; the supervisor persists that summary onto the run and emits it with supervisor_stopped so the UI can explain why the run ended.

Surfaced unattended stop summaries in the live shell. ProjectView now shows paused-state run summaries with the key counts, and recent-event summaries for supervisor_stopped include the structured stop reason instead of a generic stop line.

Hardened the live shell against stale run state: /api responses now send no-store cache headers, shared project/inbox fetches opt out of cache reuse, and the shared project store ignores out-of-order refresh responses so an older running snapshot cannot clobber a newer stopped one. Verified the backend stop summary live on /api/project against Looma/Knit; browser-use remains a noisy verifier for long-lived background refresh, so the staged throughput proof stays open.

Did a shell clarity pass on top of the runtime hardening. Thread copy is shorter, setup direction wording is plainer, and paused stop notices now summarize outcomes in operator language instead of replaying verbose orchestrator sentences with duplicated counts.

Removed another source of shell anxiety: setup phases made only of skippable steps now present as Optional, and skippable setup cards carry an explicit optional chip instead of looking like blocking prerequisites.

Quieted low-value nags further by keeping low-severity lever/policy cleanup out of the Do this next banner. Those items still exist in Notifications/Settings, but they no longer claim the primary home-shell CTA when nothing urgent is actually blocking work.

Notifications now separates urgent coordinator work from optional cleanup. High and medium items stay under Needs you; low-severity items move to Housekeeping, and the surface explicitly says nothing is blocked when optional cleanup is all that remains.
Thread now gets optional setup out of the first viewport. If setup only contains skippable steps, that phase collapses by default instead of opening as though it were blocking work.

A grounded Looma/Knit task exposed a different unattended-throughput blocker: the spec-agent had enough context and did use tools, but spent too many turns on read-only repo research and hit the max-turn limit without producing a brief, question, spec, or scoped escalation. Added a shared query-engine guardrail for repeated research-only tool turns, and wired the spec-agent to treat update-task, update-product-brief, post-user-question, and raise-escalation as the durable outcomes it must reach once evidence is sufficient.

Follow-up hardening landed in two more places. First, if a spec turn hits the max-turn limit after already writing durable task state, Guildhall now preserves that progress instead of escalating over it; writing a spec from exploring without an explicit status now promotes the task to spec_review. Second, startup session hydration is now stricter: a fresh task no longer resumes an old pending spec conversation unless the persisted snapshot names the same task id. Live Looma/Knit restart confirmed the cross-task spec-session bleed is gone, but a cold-start local NVIDIA run on task-006 still hit the spec turn limit with no durable output, so the next blocker is the model/provider path itself on fresh intake.

Small shell cleanup on top: Thread width is stable again. The column had been styled with only max-width, which allowed it to collapse narrower than intended; it now uses a fixed 680px target with max-width: 100% so the desktop reading width stays consistent without breaking mobile.

Recovered the next cold-start intake failure shape on the NVIDIA-backed Looma/Knit path. First, the spec-agent was sending near-miss tool payloads like append-exploring-transcript { item: ... } and update-product-brief { productBrief: ... }, so those tools now recover nested/stringified payloads instead of rejecting them. Then, after the model still burned turns on repeated read-only research, the query loop was hardened to refuse further read-only tool calls once a durable-progress nudge has already been issued. Live proof: fresh Looma/Knit task-008 reached spec_review in one one_task pass on qwen/qwen3.5-122b-a10b. Remaining polish: fallback brief inference needed to stop treating evidence preambles as the userJob, so that heuristic was tightened too.

Live unattended-run proof exposed a shell-level throughput bug: when a worker task entered in_progress and started using the shell tool, /api/project timed out because the Node server was blocked inside a synchronous child-process spawn. Fixed the shell tool to execute asynchronously via spawn so worker/gate commands no longer freeze the serve loop, while keeping the bootstrap runner on a separate sync helper for now. Added async shell coverage alongside the existing sync behavior tests; next live check is that project status remains responsive during an active worker turn.

Tracked down a Notifications route crash on Looma/Knit. The inbox endpoint itself was healthy, but InboxTab keyed rows by kind+title, and several open escalations shared the same title. Svelte threw each_key_duplicate, which left the route showing Loading forever. Inbox row keys now include escalation/task identity, and a small web-lib regression test covers duplicate-title escalations.

The same Notifications debugging pass exposed stale local-shell caching: after a rebuild/restart, the browser could still reuse an older /web/app.js and replay already-fixed client crashes. Tightened the SPA shell and web-asset responses to no-store/no-cache and appended a build-derived ?v= stamp to app.js/app.css in dashboardHtml so reloads pick up the live bundle.

Finished the Notifications repair in the live Looma/Knit shell. InboxTab now reuses the shell's already-fetched inbox state instead of maintaining a second fetch lifecycle, so the badge count and page body stay in sync. Verified at /notifications: Loading is gone, repeated escalation titles render safely, and the inbox rows appear immediately.

Live Looma/Knit proof on task-008 now confirms the review-fallback hardening: reviewer throttling no longer fabricates a worker revision. The task advanced review -> gate_check via reviewer-deterministic-fallback, and the latest worker self-critique was enough to reconcile acceptanceCriteria as met. The next blocker is one lane later: gate-checker-agent still stops the one-task run on NVIDIA HTTP 429 instead of preserving gate_check as a retryable provider-throttle state.

Followed the live task-008 proof one step further. Reviewer throttling is now handled well enough to preserve review truth and advance into gate_check, so the next hardening slice is gate-checker behavior under the same NVIDIA 429 pressure. The runtime is being updated so retryable provider throttles during gate_check become a resumable provider-backoff outcome rather than a generic agent-error, keeping gate_check as the truthful task state and making one-task stop summaries less alarming.

The live Looma/Knit proof finally crossed the next bar. After the reviewer-fallback hardening, task-008 was able to replay from gate_check and finish a one-task run to done on the NVIDIA provider. This is now a real grounded task that reached spec/review/gate/done through Guildhall, though the remaining trust gap is still project gate truth: the workspace root bootstrap has no authoritative hard gates, so completion currently relies on the empty gate list rather than knit/web-specific verification commands.

The unattended-throughput proof has moved from runtime noise to truthful blocked-state handling. Task-009 reached gate_check cleanly, and the next hardening slice is distinguishing task-scoped green verification from unrelated repo-wide red tests so longer unattended runs can skip honest project blockers instead of treating them as task failures.

Latest live throughput proof crossed another milestone: Looma/Knit task-009 replayed from gate_check to done in one fresh one_task run on DeepInfra Qwen 3.6 after the gate-check session freshness fix, narrowed gate normalization, and update-task metadata inference landed together.

The staged throughput proof now covers another real-world shape: multi-repo workspaces where the workspace root is not the git root. The orchestrator now drives worktree setup, base-branch lookup, merge dispatch, and cleanup through the task's effective project repo, which is a prerequisite for truthful unattended publication in Looma/Knit-style installs.

Closed a truthfulness gap before the next throughput proof: run-gates now treats the task-scoped current_task_success_gates list as authoritative at execution time, so stale model-copied bootstrap/spec commands cannot sneak back into gate_check and produce false-pass records.
